title: Complex Temperature-dependent Thermal Conductivity in a Sawtooth Chain Magnet Fe$_\mathrm{2}$SiSe$_\mathrm{4}$
authors: Kunya Yang, Feihao Pan, Liran Wang, Chenglin Shang, Ying Zhu, Xiancai Hu, Sanjiang He, Xinrun Mi, Long Zhang, Aifeng Wang, Yisheng Chai, Frederic Hardy, Christoph Meingast, Peng Cheng, Mingquan He
abstract: Geometrically frustrated magnets provide an ideal platform for exploring the interplay between lattice geometry and spin degrees of freedom. Here, we investigate the interactions between lattice and spin via thermal-transport measurements on the triangular sawtooth-lattice olivine magnet Fe$_\mathrm{2}$SiSe$_\mathrm{4}$, which exhibits successive magnetic transitions at $T_1 = 110$ K (antiferromagnetic) and $T_2 = 50$ K (ferrimagnetic). Although phonons dominate the thermal conductivity, its temperature dependence displays a pronounced double-peak structure arising from spin-phonon coupling. In the intermediate temperature range between $T_1$ and $T_2$ , resonant scattering of phonons by magnetic excitations around 5 meV produces a broad maximum around 60 K. Below $T_2$, the resonant spin-phonon scattering is strongly suppressed, leading to a rapid increase in thermal conductivity upon cooling and a pronounced low-temperature peak near 11 K, characteristic of heat transport governed by conventional phonon scattering mechanisms. Notably, this low-temperature peak is enhanced by a factor of $\sim 5$ compared to the broad maximum at higher temperatures. These results demonstrate the strong sensitivity of thermal transport to spin-lattice interactions and highlight spin-phonon scattering as an effective mechanism for tailoring thermal conductivity in geometrically frustrated magnets.
summary: This work investigates the thermal conductivity of $	ext{Fe}_2	ext{SiSe}_4$, showing that its temperature dependence is strongly dictated by magnetic ordering. The material exhibits a characteristic double-peak structure in $\kappa(T)$, which arises from resonant phonon scattering when the magnet is partially ordered, and a highly enhanced peak below the lower transition temperature due to the suppression of this spin-phonon coupling.
main_problem: The study investigates how spin fluctuations interact with lattice vibrations to influence thermal transport in a geometrically frustrated magnet.
main_result: Thermal conductivity exhibits a pronounced double-peak structure, demonstrating that resonant phonon scattering by magnetic excitations causes a broad maximum at higher temperatures, while the suppression of this coupling below the ordering temperature leads to a significantly enhanced low-temperature peak.
method: The research relies on comprehensive measurements of thermal conductivity ($\kappa(T)$) alongside magnetization and specific heat capacity across various magnetic phases.
model_or_system: The system is the triangular sawtooth-lattice olivine magnet $	ext{Fe}_2	ext{SiSe}_4$, which undergoes successive magnetic transitions at $T_1=110 	ext{ K}$ (antiferromagnetic) and $T_2=50 	ext{ K}$ (ferrimagnetic). The analysis models phonon heat transport using the Debye–Callaway model.
key_observables: Thermal conductivity $\kappa(T)$, magnetic transition temperatures ($T_1, T_2$), and the temperature dependence of magnetization $M(T)$.
important_parameters: The resonant scattering gap ($\Delta \approx 5.2 	ext{ meV}$), the enhancement factor of $\sim 5$ between the two peaks, and the transition temperatures ($110 	ext{ K}, 50 	ext{ K}$).
main_assumptions: Phonons are assumed to dominate thermal conductivity, and the scattering mechanisms (conventional vs. resonant spin-phonon) can be separated and modeled independently.
figures_summary: Figures show magnetization curves revealing magnetic transitions at $T_1$ and $T_2$, and $\kappa(T)$ plots exhibiting a distinct double-peak structure corresponding to different magnetic regimes.
paper_structure: The paper analyzes the thermal conductivity across three temperature regions defined by magnetic ordering: paramagnetic ($T>T_1$), single-$q$ ordered ($T_2 < T < T_1$), and double-$q$ ordered ($T<T_2$). Each region is analyzed using the Debye–Callaway model to quantify different scattering rates.
why_interesting: The direct measurement of how magnetic ordering modifies phonon transport provides a clear experimental signature of spin-lattice coupling, which can be modeled using concepts from open quantum systems or coupled Hamiltonian approaches in condensed matter theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a–c) Crystal and magnetic structures of Fe2SiSe4. Two inequivalent Fe sites (Fe1 and Fe2) form a sawtooth-chain lattice. A single-q and a double-q magnetic structure emerge below 𝑇1 = 110 K and 𝑇2 = 50 K, respectively. (d–f) Temperature-dependent magnetization measured under zero-field-cooled (ZFC) and field-cooled (FC) protocols in a magnetic field of 0.01 T applied along different axes. Measurements were performed upon warming (g–i) Isothermal magnetization measured with the magnetic field applied along the three crystallographic directions.

FIG. 2. (a) Temperature dependence of the specific heat 𝐶p (solid circles) measured upon warming in 0 and 9 T (𝐵∥𝑐) . The purple solid line represents the fitted phonon background using the Debye–Einstein model described in Eq. 1. (b) Magnetic specific heat 𝐶mag (blue line) obtained by subtracting the phonon contribution 𝐶ph from the total 𝐶p in zero magnetic field. The corresponding magnetic entropy 𝑆mag is also shown (orange line). (c,d) Linear thermal expansion (Δ𝐿𝑖/𝐿𝑖, 𝑖= 𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐) and the corresponding thermal-expansion coefficient [𝛼𝑖= (1/𝐿𝑖)𝑑𝐿𝑖/𝑑𝑇] measured along the three principal crystallographic axes upon warming. The inset in (d) compares the thermal-expansion coefficient along...

FIG. 3. (a) Temperature dependence of the in-plane thermal conductivity 𝜅𝑥𝑥of Fe2SiSe4, measured with the heat current applied along the chain direction (JQ ∥𝑏). A pronounced double-peak structure is observed in 𝜅𝑥𝑥(𝑇). Inset: enlarged view of the broad maximum around 60 K. The purple line denotes the electronic thermal conductivity 𝜅el, multiplied by a factor of 105 for visibility. (b) Fits to the temperature-dependent thermal conductivity using the Debye–Callaway model. In regions I and III, the data are well described by considering only the conventional phonon scattering rate 1/𝜏0.
